headline: The Humanoid Makers Reach for Wall Street
deck: Agility Robotics is going public through a $2.5 billion SPAC, the first pure-play humanoid maker on a US exchange, while Unitree has filed to list in Shanghai. The robots are still learning to walk a shift; their makers are already learning to raise from the public.
body: Agility Robotics is heading to the public markets. The Oregon maker of the warehouse humanoid Digit agreed to merge with Churchill Capital Corp XI in a deal valuing it near $2.5 billion, with a Foxconn-led investment in the accompanying private placement, expected to deliver more than $620 million in gross proceeds and to close in the second half of 2026 [E1]. Digit is already working in Amazon and MercadoLibre operations against more than $300 million in orders, and when it lists Agility will be the only US-listed company whose entire business is humanoid robots, the first pure-play the retail market can buy [E1].

China's volume leader is moving the same way from the other side of the Pacific. Unitree, which shipped more than 5,500 humanoids in 2025, filed for a listing on Shanghai's STAR Market and is seeking to raise roughly $608 million [E2]. Two of the field's busiest builders are reaching for public capital within weeks of each other, on two exchanges, under two very different sets of rules.

The timing is not a coincidence so much as a consequence. Memory priced its own record this week when SK Hynix closed its Nasdaq debut up 13 percent, and the same AI-infrastructure demand that lifts the chips is what the robot makers are selling to investors: a claim that embodied machines are about to need building at scale [E1][E2]. Capital is being raised against a fleet that mostly still runs in pilots.

That gap is the whole risk. Agility's proceeds are earmarked for product development and expansion, which is another way of saying the money arrives before the volume does, and Unitree's shipment lead is measured in thousands, not the millions its forecasters promise [E1][E2]. A public listing converts a demo into a disclosure schedule, which is useful precisely because it forces the numbers into the open.

The near-term tells are procedural and unglamorous. Whether Agility's merger clears its regulatory and shareholder votes, whether Unitree's STAR listing prices where it hopes, and whether either company's delivered-unit count grows faster than its losses will say more than any launch-day valuation [E1][E2]. The robots are learning to work a shift; their makers just filed to be judged every quarter [E1].

dissent.argument: A SPAC and a STAR filing are financing events, not demand. Pure-play scarcity can inflate the first ticker regardless of shipments; the number that matters is delivered units against cash burn, and that is still small.

evidence_box.fragment: Agility Robotics to merge with Churchill Capital Corp XI in a ~$2.5bn SPAC deal, ~$620m gross proceeds, closing H2 2026; the only US-listed pure-play humanoid company
Unitree, which shipped more than 5,500 humanoids in 2025, filed for a Shanghai STAR Market listing seeking to raise about $608 million
